title: SEGMENT 10 — Amyris and biojet fuels: the biofuels-to-cosmetics pivot

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Amyris and biojet fuels: the biofuels-to-cosmetics pivot
Welcome back.
I want to start the second half with a story that captures, in miniature, what happens when an ambitious metabolic engineering project meets the real economics of commodity chemicals.
The company is Amyris, founded in 2003 by several people from Jay Keasling's UC Berkeley lab, including Jack Newman, Kinkead Reiling, and Neil Renninger, along with John Melo as CEO.

The original mission was, in the broadest possible terms, to use the same yeast engineering platform that had produced artemisinic acid to produce other terpenoid-derived molecules at industrial scale.
The early target was a fifteen-carbon terpenoid called farnesene.
Farnesene, like artemisinic acid, is built from the mevalonate pathway by way of farnesyl pyrophosphate.

By installing a single enzyme — farnesene synthase, borrowed from an apple — and pushing flux through the engineered mevalonate pathway, Amyris yeast could be made to secrete farnesene into the fermentation broth.
Farnesene is a hydrocarbon, oil-soluble, and it floats on top of the aqueous broth, where it can be skimmed off.
That is a beautiful product geometry for downstream processing — no expensive purification, just decant the oil.
The ambition was huge.
Farnesene is a long-chain hydrocarbon that, after hydrogenation to farnesane, has properties similar to diesel fuel.
Amyris went after the biofuels market — biojet, bio-diesel — pitched as a renewable alternative to petroleum fuels, with the major airline industry as a target customer.
They built fermentation capacity in Brazil, where sugarcane provided a cheap fermentable feedstock, and they raised hundreds of millions of dollars on the biofuels vision.
Then the economics happened.
Petroleum prices stayed low.
Microbial biofuel production, even at the impressive yields Amyris achieved, remained substantially more expensive per liter than petroleum-derived diesel.
The market for renewable jet fuel existed but was small, willing to pay only modest premiums, and dominated by competing approaches like used cooking oil.
Amyris could not compete on price in the bulk fuel market.
The whole biofuels-from-engineered-microbes movement of the late 2000s — Amyris, LS9, Solazyme, Gevo, several others — collectively bumped up against the same reality.
You could make fuel-grade hydrocarbons from sugar in engineered cells; you just could not make them cheaper than petroleum.
So Amyris pivoted.
They noticed that farnesene, hydrogenated to farnesane, was chemically very similar to a high-value cosmetic ingredient called squalane.

Squalane is a saturated hydrocarbon traditionally extracted from shark liver oil — a horrifying supply chain in environmental terms, decimating shark populations — and used as a skin emollient in high-end cosmetics.
Demand for squalane was strong, prices were high (orders of magnitude above fuel prices per liter), and there was an obvious appetite for a renewable, animal-free alternative.
Amyris took their farnesane and rebranded it as biosqualane, sold it into the cosmetics industry, and found a profitable margin.
The same molecule that had been a biofuel commodity became a premium cosmetic ingredient.
The pivot worked.

The analogy I want you to carry: Amyris is the biofuels-to-cosmetics pivot — they started making biojet fuel from engineered yeast, couldn't beat petroleum on price, and discovered that the same farnesene molecule was a great squalane replacement for the cosmetics industry.
They pivoted from a commodity market they could not win to a specialty market they could.
It is a pattern that recurs all over metabolic engineering.
Microbial production tends to be expensive per kilogram compared to entrenched alternatives.

The successful applications are usually high-value specialty chemicals — drugs, fragrances, cosmetic ingredients, food additives — where the per-kilogram premium of microbial production can be justified by the value of the product, the environmental story, or the avoidance of a problematic supply chain.
Amyris kept building out the platform.
They expanded into other terpenoid products — flavors, fragrances, vitamins, more cosmetic ingredients — and into specialty food ingredients via spin-off and partnership arrangements.
Their financial history has been turbulent, with the company going through restructuring as recently as the mid-2020s, but the strain engineering platform itself produced real products at real scale.
The lesson for the field was clear.
Pick your markets carefully.
Microbial production wins where the alternative is more expensive, more polluting, or based on a finite or ethically problematic source.
It loses in commodity markets where it competes directly with cheap petrochemicals.
The successful metabolic engineering companies of the next decade, in many cases, learned from the Amyris experience and went straight to specialty markets, skipping the biofuels detour.
We will, in the very last segment, come back to whether the biofuels dream might yet revive if we start counting carbon properly and if cells can be engineered to fix CO2 directly.
But for the commercial story up to about 2020, the lesson is: pick specialty, not commodity.
With that lesson in mind, let us look at the company that turned strain engineering itself into a horizontal platform — Ginkgo Bioworks.
